data.title: INmune Bio: Finding Pay Dirt In Alzheimer's?
data.description:
Topline Summary and Update

INmune Bio (NASDAQ:INMB) is an immunology/neurology developmental-focused biotech that has been the subject of substantial scrutiny, speculation, and discussion on Seeking Alpha. Last December, I highlighted the then-imminent readout of a key trial the company was conducting in Alzheimer's disease. Now we have a definitive date for the findings, and the market has gone haywire, sending the stock price up as much as 70% and then falling substantially. Today, let's take a look at what's about to happen, and whether now is the time to make a quick move.

Pipeline Updates

XPro

The main project of focus for INMB remains XPro, a dominant negative regulator of tumor necrosis factor alpha (TNFa). The thought process here is that soluble TNFa is thought to contribute to inflammation of the nervous system, in turn promoting neurodegenerative disorders like Alzheimer's disease. I've covered at some length the preliminary signals that suggest XPro can modify the Alzheimer's disease trajectory, with the caveat that nothing clinically definitive has emerged yet. You can read more about that here.

The big news is that, finally, INMB has announced that they would be sharing topline findings from the phase 2 MINDFuL trial of XPro treatment in patients with early Alzheimer's and biomarkers associated with neuroinflammation. It is hoped that we're going to get a readout of cognitive effects associated with XPro treatment in this setting.

We already know from the company that one scale to measure cognitive decline, EMACC, has been improved in the study. So a full analysis should hopefully give us a clearer picture of the future of XPro in this indication. For reference, the pivotal trial for lecanemab from 2023 used the Clinical Dementia Rating–Sum of Boxes (CDR-SB) score as its primary endpoint. The least-squares mean change from baseline to 18 months of the CDR-SB was 1.21 with lecanemab compared with 1.66 with placebo (higher numbers indicate worse score.

CDR is a secondary endpoint for the MINDFuL study, and EMACC is the primary endpoint, but the aforementioned previous work from INMB has demonstrated favorable correlation between EMACC and CDR-SB.

What will this data readout show? It's anyone's best guess. Some will look to the timing of the presentation (8 AM on a Monday) as a good sign, since it indicates building substantial momentum right at the start of a trading week. Others might look to the dilutive equity raise announced a day after the announcement of the upcoming presentation as a sign that the results can't be that good. Otherwise, INMB would have more likely attempted to dovetail the announced results with the funding.

CORDStrom

Another program that INMB is working on is the pooled mesenchymal stem cell product dubbed CORDStrom. The first disease target for this project is recessive dystrophic epidermolysis bullosa (RDEB), a congenital disorder affecting around 4,000 people in the U.S., U.K., and EU.

Back in February, the company announced findings from the MissionEB study, a randomized, double-blind study including 30 children with intermediate or severe RDEB. CORDStrom treatment was associated with a substantial, sustained reduction in itch. Treatment was very well tolerated as well.

After a Type C meeting with the FDA, INMB announced their intention to submit a Biologics Licensing Application by the end of the year, targeting approval and a potential drug launch in 2026. CORDStrom has Orphan Drug designation, which grants marketing exclusivity and greater attention from the FDA.

Financial Overview

As of their latest quarterly filing, INMB held $19.3 million in cash and equivalents. Operating expenses for Q1 were about $10 million, and after other income, the net loss for the quarter reached $9.7 million. Actual cash burn was $6.8 million.

At this burn rate, INMB maintains liquidity sufficient to fund almost 3 quarters of ongoing operations. However, on the back of their announcement of upcoming data, INMB announced a registered direct offering intended to bring in $19 million, selling 3 million shares at $6.30 per share. Assuming this is successful, this equity raise should buy another quarter's liquidity, hopefully sufficient to get CORDStrom approved.

Strengths and Risks

Strength - A diverse pipeline, including a few programs that may be ready to head toward approval

We know with some certainty that INMB is going to be advancing their CORDStrom program toward approval in the rare RDEB indication, for which the company sees a market opportunity of around $1 billion. I also didn't touch on updates surrounding INKmune, the natural killer cell program being explored as a treatment for prostate cancer (you can read more on that as of December in my last article).

Risk - Is XPro good to go or not?

But the wildcard piece is obviously XPro, and we've seen a few positive signals emerge out of the study to date. But these are mostly correlative (biomarkers of inflammation, much discussed imaging findings suggesting impact on gray matter), and we have not seen any sliver of data on cognitive decline, which is the main point of the MINDFuL study.

What INMB has done to hype up this data readout isn't incredibly rare. We do see a lot of biotech companies announce that they're going to announce data. And sometimes that does pan out to be a meaningful, positive, binary event.

However, it is also logical to assume that INMB is attempting to capitalize more on the hype of possible positive results, further cemented with the announced equity raise. At this point, it is very difficult to know what's going to come out, but I would imagine that if they had in hand a clear improvement in the primary endpoint, that they would just announce it, surprise the market, and then raise funds from a massive position of strength.

Bottom Line Summary

It's certainly something to see a stock take news and move above their yearly high, only to fall to prices near their 52-week low, all on the same day. This is not the kind of roller coaster you hope to sign up for as a would-be investor. At this time, after my last "Buy" sentiment in December, I am inclined to be less optimistic about the chances for XPro.

Certainly, the data announcement could be a massive upward catalyst on Monday, but there's a lot of uncertainty here as well. Some saving grace is the positive RDEB findings we've seen and the anticipated application for approval later this year, giving INMB something else to hang its hat on if XPro turns out to need more time to mature, or even if it ends up with flatly negative results.

This pipeline flexibility, along with the potential that MINDFuL will read out positive (despite the premature-looking equity raise) makes me affirm my rating, but it is critical to take caution and not overextend past where you are comfortable with this one.

data.title: Above The Noise: Putting Pessimism In Perspective
data.description:
By Brian Levitt

Linus Van Pelt: "Life is difficult, isn't it, Charlie Brown?"Charlie Brown: "Yes, it is, but I've developed a new philosophy. I only dread one day at a time."

Our favorite blockhead may dread each day as it comes, but there’s a quiet optimism in him. He never stops trying to kick that football or to fly that kite. I can relate. This is one of those moments when I dread each day, unsure of what the next news cycle or round of messages will bring. And yet, I, too, wrestle with pessimism.

Author Mustafa Suleyman recently introduced the concept of the pessimism aversion trap, the psychological tendency to avoid engaging with negative possibilities. Don’t get me wrong, I let pessimism creep into my personal life. After all, I’m a New York Giants fan. However, my aversion to pessimism lies in how we view society’s ability to overcome challenges and build a better future. That boy has cried wolf one too many times.

Take, for example, the many doomsday predictions that never quite came to pass:

  • Baby boomers were all supposed to retire and crash the housing and stock markets.
  • We were going to run out of oil.
  • US was on the brink of defaulting on its debt.
  • A COVID-19 vaccine would take a decade.
  • 2022 was declared the start of a new era of stagflation.
  • Every office building in the country was supposedly destined to sit empty. Rest assured that my commute to the office is currently as awful as it has ever been!

Suleyman might argue that falling into the pessimism aversion trap could lead to dangerous complacency. I’m mindful of that. Still, I can’t help but contextualize the issues currently weighing on investors. Here are two examples:

  • War: The most recent Israel-Hamas war began on October 7, 2023. Since then, the S&P 500 Index has advanced by more than 40%.1
  • Trade war: In 2019, the year between the start of the US-China trade war and the 2020 trade deal, the S&P 500 Index rose 31.5%.2

As Charlie Brown also said, “Learn from yesterday, live for today, look to tomorrow, rest this afternoon.”

It may be confirmation bias, but…

…the market doesn’t believe that a recession is coming. Since the beginning of the year, US high yield corporate bonds have outperformed the S&P 500 Index.3 High yield bond spreads have tightened by more than 150 basis points since April 8, when spreads peaked.4 Also, this year, the S&P 500 Industrials sector has significantly outpaced the broader index.5

Recession? The market seems to think otherwise.

Since you asked (part 1)

Q: What impact could the war between Israel and Iran have on the financial markets?

A: Geopolitical conflicts and wars often trigger immediate volatility in financial markets, but investors should take a step back and ask two key questions: Does this event meaningfully alter the growth outlook for the world’s largest economies? Does it change expectations for how major central banks will respond? If the answer to both is no, then even tragic and unsettling events are unlikely to derail broader market cycles.

The thinking would only shift if something were to occur that changed the calculus. For example, a closure of the Strait of Hormuz, a vital route for global energy flows bordered to the north by Iran, could significantly disrupt oil and natural gas markets and weigh on global economic growth. However, such a scenario doesn’t appear imminent. It's also worth noting that the US is currently producing 13.4 million barrels of oil per day, a record level that provides a significant buffer against external shocks.6

Since you asked (part 2)

Q: Why are tariffs not yet showing up in the inflation data?

A: Imports into the US surged in the first quarter as businesses rushed to stockpile goods ahead of new tariffs.7 Since then, imports have declined sharply. As we move through the summer and into the fall, the impact of these tariffs is expected to appear more clearly in inflation data. If anything, the greater concern for the stock market may be if tariffs fail to show up in inflation data. This would imply that businesses are absorbing the costs, potentially hurting corporate profitability.

The encouraging longer-term view is that bond market expectations for long-term inflation remain stable, suggesting that tariffs may cause a short-term price shock rather than sustained inflation.8

It was said

“I would like to get this guy to lower interest rates because if he doesn’t, we have to pay.”

- President Donald Trump

I apologize for returning to this topic, but the independence of the US Federal Reserve (Fed) remains a critical safeguard against significantly higher interest rates on government debt. While lowering rates in response to a weakening labor market is a legitimate policy response, doing so to placate the executive branch is a far more troubling prospect. A historical example worth noting is the trajectory of US interest rates following President Nixon’s politicization of the Arthur Burns-led Fed, an episode that highlights the dangers of compromising central bank autonomy.

Currently, the average maturity of US debt is approximately six years.9 Treasury yields, particularly in the intermediate to long end of the curve, tend to reflect the broader strength of the US economy. Historically, a sharp decline in these yields has often signaled a recession, an outcome that would likely further strain the US fiscal position.

A different tune

Recent dollar weakness has prompted the usual chorus proclaiming the end of US exceptionalism.10 I choose to frame it differently. A pullback from lofty levels offers a tailwind for US multinationals and opens the door for capital to explore opportunities beyond American borders.

Everyone has a podcast

David Nadel joined our Greater Possibilities podcast to discuss investing in international small- and mid-sized (SMID) businesses. Here are some takeaways:

Reversal of long-term trends

Recent international equity strength is attributed to a reversal of the 15-year trend favoring US stocks, driven by a weakening dollar and shifting global growth dynamics.11

Valuation gap and opportunity

International SMID stocks (small- and mid-cap) are significantly undervalued compared to US counterparts, trading at a 40–50% discount on the forward price-to-earnings ratio, offering compelling upside potential.12

Rate cuts and resilience

International SMID companies may be better positioned to benefit from global rate cuts, especially in regions not facing potential US-style inflation pressures. High quality, long-duration assets in these markets potentially stand to gain.

Under allocation and misperception

US investors are under allocated to international SMID (<1%) compared to international large caps and US SMID, largely due to risk perceptions that David says are misplaced.13

On the road again

My travel has (mercifully) slowed. ‘Tis not the season for investment conferences. I’ll instead take a moment to congratulate the class of 2025. Wishing you all the best as you set out on the road ahead!

  • 1

    Source: Bloomberg L.P., June 18, 2025, based on the total return of the S&P 500 Index from October 7, 2023 to June 18, 2025.

  • 2

    Source: Bloomberg L.P., June 18, 2025, based on the 2019 total return of the S&P 500 Index.

  • 3

    Source: Bloomberg L.P., June 18, 2025, based on the year-to-date performance of the S&P 500 Index (+2.33%) and the Bloomberg US Corporate High Yield Bond Index (+3.31%) as of June 18, 2025.

  • 4

    Source: Bloomberg L.P., June 18, 2025, based on the option-adjusted spread of the Bloomberg US Corporate High Yield Bond Index from the current high of the year on April 8, 2025 to June 18, 2025.

  • 5

    Source: Bloomberg L.P., June 18, 2025, based on the year-to-date performance of the S&P 500 Industrial Sector GICS Level 1 Index (+8.32%) and the S&P 500 Index (+2.33%).

  • 6

    Source: US Department of Energy, June 13, 2025, latest data available.

  • 7

    Source: US Census Bureau, April 30, 2025.

  • 8

    Source: Bloomberg L.P., June 18, 2025, based on the 10-year US inflation breakeven. A breakeven inflation rate is a market-derived estimate of future inflation, calculated by comparing the yield on a standard government bond (nominal) to the yield on an inflation-protected bond (OTCPK:TIPS) of the same maturity.

  • 9

    Source: US Department of Treasury, May 31, 2025.

  • 10

    Source: Bloomberg L.P., June 18, 2025, based on the US Dollar Index, which measures the value of the US dollar versus a trade-weighted basket of currencies.

  • 11

    Source: Bloomberg L.P., May 31, 2025, based on the 15-year performance of the MSCI All Country World (ACWI) ex USA Index (+6.9% annualized return) and the S&P 500 Index (+14.2% annualized return).

  • 12

    Source: Bloomberg L.P., May 31, 2025, based on the price-to-earnings ratios of MSCI World ex-US Small Cap Index (19.1x) and Russell 2000 Index (49.5x).

  • 13

    Source: Investment Company Institute, May 31, 2025, based on mutual fund and ETF assets.

Important information

NA4609941

Image: Astroid / Adobe Stock

All investing involves risk, including the risk of loss.

Past performance does not guarantee future results.

Investments cannot be made directly in an index.

This does not constitute a recommendation of any investment strategy or product for a particular investor. Investors should consult a financial professional before making any investment decisions.

A basis point is one-hundredth of a percentage point.

The Bloomberg US Corporate High Yield Bond Index measures the US dollar-denominated, high yield, fixed-rate corporate bond market. Securities are classified as high yield if the middle rating of Moody’s, Fitch, and S&P is Ba1/BB+/BB+ or below.

Forward price-to-earnings (P/E) ratio is a variant of a company’s price-to-earnings ratio and is calculated by dividing the company’s current share price by its expected earnings, usually for the next 12 months or the next full fiscal year.

Fixed income investments are subject to credit risk of the issuer and the effects of changing interest rates. Interest rate risk refers to the risk that bond prices generally fall as interest rates rise and vice versa. An issuer may be unable to meet interest and/or principal payments, thereby causing its instruments to decrease in value and lowering the issuer’s credit rating.

Credit spread is the difference in yield between bonds of similar maturity but with different credit quality.

High yield bonds, or junk bonds, involve a greater risk of default or price changes due to changes in the issuer’s credit quality. The values of junk bonds fluctuate more than those of high quality bonds and can decline significantly over short time periods.

Inflation is the rate at which the general price level for goods and services is increasing.

The MSCI All Country World (ACWI) ex USA Index is an unmanaged index considered representative of large- and mid-cap stocks across developed and emerging markets, excluding the US.

The MSCI World ex USA Small Cap Index captures small-cap representation across 22 of 23 developed markets (DM) countries (excluding the US).

The risks of investing in securities of foreign issuers can include fluctuations in foreign currencies, political and economic instability, and foreign taxation issues.

The Russell 2000® Index measures the performance of small-capitalization stocks and is a trademark/service mark of the Frank Russell Co.®.

The S&P 500® Index is an unmanaged index considered representative of the US stock market.

The S&P 500® Industrials Index includes stocks in the S&P 500 Index classified as industrial companies based on the Global Industry Classification Standard methodology. The index is market-cap weighted.

The S&P 500 Industrials Sector GICS Level 1 Index represents the industrial sector within the S&P 500 Index, as defined by the Global Industry Classification Standard (GICS). It includes companies involved in capital goods, commercial and professional services, and transportation.

Stagflation is an economic condition marked by a combination of slow economic growth and rising prices.

Stocks of small- and medium-sized companies tend to be more vulnerable to adverse developments, may be more volatile, and may be illiquid or restricted as to resale.

In general, stock values fluctuate, sometimes widely, in response to activities specific to the company as well as general market, economic and political conditions.

The yield curve plots interest rates, at a set point in time, of bonds having equal credit quality but differing maturity dates to project future interest rate changes and economic activity.

The opinions referenced above are those of the author as of June 27, 2025. These comments should not be construed as recommendations, but as an illustration of broader themes. Forward-looking statements are not guarantees of future results. They involve risks, uncertainties, and assumptions; there can be no assurance that actual results will not differ materially from expectations.

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

Above The Noise: Putting Pessimism In Perspective by Invesco US

data.title: American Outdoors Is More Reasonably Priced, But Q1 Might Bring Better Prices
data.description:
American Outdoor Brands, Inc. (NASDAQ:AOUT) presented Q4 2025 earnings (ended April 2025), with very positive results, albeit with some important tailwinds and nuances.

The name is still growing (adjusted for those nuances or not), but management was very cautious about FY 2026 guidance and comments.

I believe the company is not as overvalued as it was a few months ago, pre-tariffs, but that given the environment, it is not screaming cheap, either. I prefer to maintain a conservative position and keep the Hold.

Q4 2025 Results

Revenue explosion with tailwinds: The company posted 33% revenue growth, which is impressive by any standard.

However, management clarified on the call that approximately $8 to $10 million in sales, or about 15 percentage points, were pull-forward orders from Q1 2026, as retailers accelerated buying ahead of anticipated price increases from new tariffs.

Even excluding this, revenue growth would have been around 15%, which is still excellent and aligns well with an 18% increase for the year.

Restocking tailwinds: Another tailwind not explicitly addressed in the call is the end of the destocking cycle that has affected outdoor goods since at least 2022. At some point, retailers must replenish, and it's likely that much of FY 2025 benefited from this dynamic.

AOUT had previously guided for more modest mid-single-digit growth in FY 2026, suggesting that the 15% pace is not a sustainable baseline. This doesn’t mean AOUT isn’t growing, but that growth normalization should be expected.

New product tailwind: A third and very important tailwind was the launch of several standout new products. The biggest hit was the Caldwell ClayCopter, a very innovative concept in target throwers for sport shooting.

The product was highlighted in the call as having already outsold every other clay thrower at a major retail partner. Online reviews of the product are very positive.

This clearly created a boost to both POS trends and wholesale shipments, but likely represents a non-recurring driver of growth.

Gross margins flat reveal the cost structure: Gross margins for FY 2025 were up only 60bps to 44.6%, despite the double-digit sales growth. Gross margin in Q4 2025 fell sequentially despite the 33% revenue increase, indicating that AOUT’s cost of goods is heavily variable.

This is important because it limits the company’s ability to expand gross margins even in strong sales environments. Tariffs were not a direct headwind this quarter, but their impact will increase as inventory turns bring higher-cost goods into the P&L later in FY 2026.

Very good OpEx leverage: The company did deliver excellent operating leverage on the SG&A side. Operating expenses fell from 50% to 45% of sales for the year, driven by a combination of higher revenues, lower intangible amortization, and tighter cost control.

SG&A was essentially flat for the quarter despite the surge in revenue. This is one of the most important and positive signs in the report, as it confirms the business model's ability to scale earnings.

Valuation Is Not Cheap

The company broke even on a GAAP basis in FY 2025. Adjusting for excess intangible amortization from acquisitions, FY 2025 net income comes to around $6 million. At a $150 million market cap, that implies a 25x P/E, expensive for a consumer goods name in today’s environment.

Bulls could argue that with a fixed OpEx base, modest revenue growth could rapidly expand earnings. For example, with flat 45% gross margins and OpEx at ~$90 million (excluding amortization), AOUT only needs to grow revenues by 10% to double net income to $15 million.

That argument is not unreasonable, and it’s likely that over a two-year period, earnings could grow if the company continues executing well. But the setup still requires several optimistic assumptions: flat gross margins despite tariff noise, sustained innovation, and stable consumer spending in a discretionary category, plus no effect from the inventory cycle.

I believe the stock only works under those favorable scenarios. I prefer to wait for a lower entry point where expectations are reduced and upside/downside is more skewed in investors’ favor.

Next quarter might be that chance: With $8–10 million of Q1 2026 sales pulled forward into Q4 2025, and with management previously guiding for MSD revenue growth in Q1 2026, it's reasonable to expect revenue could fall 20 percentage points YoY in the upcoming quarter. This kind of headline might trigger some fear-driven selling, and could offer a more attractive entry point.

Until then, I remain a Hold.

data.title: Arbor Realty Trust: Don't Be Fooled By The 11.5% Dividend Yield
data.description:
Thesis

Arbor Realty's (NYSE:ABR) stock has been disappointing lately, recently hitting a five-year low.

There seem to be several reasons why the stock is struggling on the NYSE. Some of them include the Fed's delay in cutting interest rates, weak financial results, and a 30% dividend cut.

The last point, in fact, carries a lot of weight for a stock that has traditionally offered a very high dividend, which has been its main strength in attracting investors, as we'll see further below.

These factors have not only dragged the stock down, but have also led to a sharp increase in short interest, now at an impressive 31.5%, with an average cover period of 10 days.

As the analysis will show, I can only give a "HOLD" recommendation, even though ABR appears cheap in terms of valuation, has a fairly high-quality real estate portfolio (mostly multifamily and single-family rentals), and its management has announced some interesting developments and a solid portfolio overall.

Stock Overview

The ABR has been moving sideways for the last three years. The chart shows that it has clearly lost touch with the all-time highs it reached in late 2021, having dropped by 50%.

The $15.90 price level retreat by buyers during the fall of 2024 carried dual significance. The price drop at this level produced both a short-term trading signal which remains active and validated the ongoing downward market trend.

The stock price should move toward $7 because of its downward trend and the negative market momentum that affects ABR.

The stock maintained positive returns for investors who maintained their position during the entire period.

The stock price has increased by 56% throughout the last decade while experiencing major losses. The total return reaches +326% when you add dividend payments to the stock's performance because this defensive stock maintains a beta of 0.75.

The Russell 2000 small-cap index which contains ABR has only achieved a 65% return during this time period.

Dividend Cut

The company's long-term success depends heavily on its dividend payments. The dividend payment rate remains high at $0.30 per share with an 11.5% yield as of the May 16 ex-dividend date.

The total yield for 2025 will increase to 12.7% because ABR maintained its Q3 and Q4 dividend payments at $0.30 while it paid $0.43 in Q1 before reducing it by 30% to $0.30.

ABR has maintained a reliable regular dividend payment structure throughout its history without any special dividend distributions.

The company has maintained a consistent dividend increase pattern in addition to its high yield, which remains substantial.

The annual dividend payment started at $0.52 in 2014 before reaching $1.72 in 2024. I do not believe this trend will persist throughout this year.

The main interest of investors who want to invest in this stock type should focus on dividend yield.

A person who plans to hold stocks for the long term normally changes their investment plans if the company demonstrates poor performance during the first few quarters.

The investor should evaluate alternative options when a company demonstrates signs of trouble because frequent trading goes against long-term investment principles.

The current situation of ABR does not indicate major trouble, but the company's performance remains unsatisfactory. The current market conditions do not support new stock purchases, so the rating maintains its hold position. The 30% dividend reduction combined with declining stock prices and weak market demand makes it difficult to consider purchasing this stock at present.

Soft Financials and Mixed Portfolio Performance

The company reported a 48% decrease in net income year-over-year to $30.4 million ($0.16 per diluted share) from $57.9 million ($0.3 per share) in the same period last year while distributable earnings decreased to $57.3 million ($0.28 per share) compared to $96.7 million ($0.47 per share) in Q12024.

The portfolio presents a combination of different investment types. The company maintains a secure business model because its loan portfolio consists of more than 95% multifamily and single-family rental properties which experience stable occupancy rates and housing shortages throughout various regions.

In Q1 2025, the structured loan portfolio stood at $11.49 billion, following $747.1 million in new originations and $421.9 million in repayments. The company executed seven non-performing loan foreclosures, which resulted in $196.7 million worth of real estate owned properties.

Arbor achieved an innovative $802 million securitization deal that focused on build-to-rent projects. The financing agreement provides a 2.48% interest margin above SOFR with a revolving feature that enables capital reinvestment. The company used its funds to pay off two higher-cost CLO structures, which released approximately $80 million of additional liquidity.

One weakness was loan sales to Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac that fell extremely in Q1 2025 from $1.3 billion to $605 million. This happened due to market slow down, with higher rates and less demand for mortgage-backed deals. The company announced $310 million in cash and total debt of under $10 billion.

Subdued Performance Expectations

As we saw above, the extended drop in the stock is clearly justified.

The huge short interest, estimated at over 31%, adds to that picture, showing how negative the market's expectations are. Of course, the counterpoint here is that if these short positions start closing, the stock could get a lift, since covering shorts involves buying shares, often in a rush. Still, overall, the outlook does not look great.

Personally, I do not like investing in stocks that are in a strong downtrend. I do not believe in stories like "I am the only one who sees the bargain here."

On the contrary, if a stock looks this weak, there is usually a reason, and some of those reasons I have already discussed in this article. There might be others I have not seen.

That said, if someone takes a different view and looks for outsized returns in what might still be an interesting name like ABR, a small position could be justified, as long as they are careful with how much of their portfolio they put in, and they are aware of the risk they are taking for the chance of a bigger reward.

For the record, the stock trades at just 6.7x earnings, that is 40% below the sector average P/E, while also offering one of the highest dividend yields among REITs (and beyond).

Conclusion

ABR is going through a rough time on the New York Stock Exchange. Even though it might look attractive at these levels, I still think there is a good chance the sideways decline will continue, and I do not see a strong enough reason in terms of risk and return.

Also, the weak financial results, which led to a major 30% dividend cut, hurt the stock's image, mainly because the dividend has been its biggest strength over the years.

In fact, the higher yield of 11.5% is only there because of the combination of a still-decent payout and the sharp drop in the stock price, which is now down 50% from its 2021 highs.

data.title: Titan America: Capital Cycle Changes Merit A Hold
data.description:
Belgian manufacturer and supplier of heavy building materials, Titan America SA (NYSE:TTAM) operates in an expanding industry, growing supply in response to economic conditions. The company’s exposure to cementitious solutions could unlock tremendous value, while its location in an attractive region presents favorable tailwinds. Despite the firm’s growth and profitability, considering the questions around its capital cycle, the impact of its relationship with its parent company, and the extent of growth the company will experience, I believe the stock merits a Hold recommendation.

Industry Supply is Growing

Titan America operates in an industry in which capital expenditure decisions are separated by large time lags from price observations. In other words, the time between establishing a cement import terminal, for instance, and selling that cement at the prevailing price. This leads to the risk that management will overestimate future prices, and therefore, overinvest in capacity. When this occurs at an industry level, excess supply leads to a collapse in prices, an exit of capital, and returns on invested capital (ROIC) falling toward the weighted average cost of capital (WACC), until the market is cleared. It is noteworthy, then, that Titan America’s ROIC has levitated around 13.9% in the last three years, moving from 13.26% in 2023 to 14.78% in 2024 to 13.65% in the last twelve months (LTM). The decline in ROIC in the LTM explains the share price’s 23.89% decline since its Feb. 3 2025 initial public offering (IPO). The firm’s capital intensity, which I measure as capital expenditure divided by revenue, has declined from 10.06% in 2022 to 8.16% in the LTM, indicating increasingly disciplined capacity expansion post-2022.

The increase in capital intensity aligns with the firm’s increase in capex, which in turn follows a broader industry shift toward increased capital expenditure. Heidelberg Materials AG (OTCPK:HLBZF), the world’s second-largest cement maker, has committed to raising its capex, believing that the infrastructure boom allows it to do so while raising its ROIC. India’s UltraTech Cement Limited has also raised its capex for the next three years, aiming to spend some $3.9 billion. Globally, there is a move to increase supply, in response to supply constraints, friend-shoring needs, and rising populations, and with this comes the risk of overinvestment, a sharp decline in prices, and reduced margins.

Supplementary Cementitious Materials Could Unlock Value

Since 2022, revenue has compounded by a modest 6.05% a year, at a time in which the cement industry’s prospects have appeared grim, and characterized by declining volumes, and low valuations. Titan America’s ability to grow through this era stems from their offering of heavy building materials that contribute to decarbonization, as well as the firm’s reuse of waste materials.

Titan America’s adoption of supplementary cementitious materials (SCM) and fillers has counteracted the negative trends in cement economics. Management explained in the 2024 20-F that,

Our portfolio includes high-performance concrete and cementitious products tailored to meet increasingly demanding construction needs for infrastructure and commercial markets. High-strength and high-modulus elasticity concrete is designed for increasingly tall and slender high-rise buildings in major urban markets. High-durability concretes are developed for projects requiring extended service life in extreme conditions, including landmark tunnels and bridges. These products include marine and underwater concretes designed for saltwater exposure. Our GreenCrete® product line provides independently validated less carbon-intensive concrete mixtures (based on third-party measurements of embodied CO2 content relative to industry benchmark specifications) while enhancing performance across all applications. Our lower carbon and high-performing cements and supplementary cementitious materials are critical to enable these concrete mixes. We believe that our high-performance products not only enhance the quality of construction but also support sustainable and resilient development.

A report by McKinsey shows that Titan America’s observations represent the basis of a potential “golden age” in the industry: not only are these cementitious solutions low-carbon, but they are also cheaper, helping to alleviate supply-side problems in growing states. Regional growth, the rising cost of cement and concrete products, regional supplier consolidation, and a shift to new solutions and business models, support the growth of cementitious solutions. As the product mix shifts more decisively in this direction, rather than an industry expectation of reduced margins, margins should increase or at least stabilize.

Agglomeration Economies Help Titan America

Operating largely on the Eastern Seaboard of the United States, Titan America is present in Florida, the New York and New Jersey Metropolitan area, Virginia, North Carolina, and South Carolina. Florida acts as its own segment, while the other states are bundled under the “Mid-Atlantic” segment. The company serves markets defined by high population growth, with Florida, South Carolina, and New Jersey being among the fifteen fastest-growing states in the country; rapid economic growth, with Florida and South Carolina being among the fifteen states in terms of GDP growth; and technological and innovation advances, with the Consumer Technology Association (CTA) rating South Dakota as an “innovation champion”.

As the agglomeration economies enjoyed by these states increase, their attractiveness rises, creating a virtuous cycle in which Titan America can benefit through continued growth and attractive profits. The regional focus ensures that management can rapidly shift resources to where they are most productive, aiding the firm in its quest to earn attractive profits.

Titan Cement’s Ownership is an Asset

Greek cement and building materials' producer, Titan Cement International SA, controls 87% of Titan America’s voting power. The implication is clear: Titan America’s public shareholders will have limited influence in its affairs, with Titan Cement controlling its strategic decisions, board appointments, and capital allocation, creating the palpable risk that Titan Cement acts with impunity in ways at odds with its public shareholders. Akin to this is how Meta Platforms, Inc.'s (META) voting structure insulates Mark Zuckerberg from public scrutiny. I use Meta as an example to make a different kind of point: while risks are certainly created, those risks can turn out to be a benefit, when they insulate intelligent management from the market’s short-term thinking, freeing it to make long-term capital allocation decisions. Moreover, Titan America, as a controlled company, benefits from strategic alignment with its parent company, financial backing from a more long-sighted backer, and synergies with Titan Cement, which will help improve margins or market share in the long run.

Growth Has Come with Profitability

As aforementioned, the company enjoys a 3-year sales CAGR of 6.05%. Not only has the firm grown, but it has earned attractive profits, with net operating profit after tax (NOPAT) rising from $177.66 million in 2023 to $194.16 million in the LTM, compounding by 4.54% a year. NOPAT margin in that time has averaged 11.71%, being 11.16% in 2023, 12.03% in 2024, and 11.94% in the LTM. Balance sheet efficiency has declined in that time, with invested capital turns to be 1.19 in 2023, 1.23 in 2024, and 1.14 in the LTM. The decline in NOPAT margins and invested capital turns explains not just the decline in ROIC that I have already discussed, but also the share price decline -nearly 29% since the February 2 IPO. The decline is also a feature of the firm’s free cash flow (FCF), which has declined from $275.38 million in 2023 to $89.8 million in the LTM. At the present valuation, the FCF enjoys an attractive yield of 3.42%.

The Market Expects a 51% Increase in NOPAT

At the present share price, $12.71 at the time of writing, the company has a price-to-economic book value of 1.51, implying that the market expects its NOPAT to rise by 51% from the current NOPAT. Using my reverse discounted cash flow (DCF) model, I teased out the expectations implied by the share price.

In the base scenario, I determined the conditions under which the share price is justified, wherein,

  • Revenue grows by 3.55% in 2025, 8.44% in 2026, 8.05% in 2027, 4.74% in 2028, and 6.64% in 2029, in line with Seeking Alpha’s consensus estimates, and then by 15% thereafter, and,

  • Titan America maintains its LTM NOPAT margin of 11.94%.

In this scenario, the company has a market-implied competitive advantage period (MICAP) of 10 years, at which the shareholder value per share equals the current price.

An upside can be seen if:

  • Revenue grows by an average of 10%, and,

  • NOPAT margin rises to an average of 14.08%.

Under this scenario, the company is worth $13.53, an upside of 6.45%. I think this demonstrates the most likely optimistic scenario for Titan America, which is a rather modest return. There is a catastrophist scenario possible, in which,

  • Revenue grows by 6.05% a year, the firm’s 3-year sales CAGR, and,

  • NOPAT margin continues at the current level.

Under this scenario, the impact of future decarbonization and the touted infrastructure boom is not visible, and the future resembles the past. This would be disastrous for shareholders, with the stock worth $6.33 today, a downside of 50.2% from the present price.

Conclusion

The picture we have drawn is one in which the capital cycle is potentially unfavorable, with industry supply rising and firms across the world increasing capex. The value unlocked by cementitious solutions could counteract these effects, carrying as they do the possibility of greater revenues and NOPAT margins. Titan America also benefits from operating in an attractive region, and insulation from short-termism. It should also be noted that the firm’s existence as a controlled company not only potentially insulates the firm, but is also a potential risk, and it is hard to know for sure what the long-run effects will be. Titan America has grown and done so profitably, and the market has rewarded it with an attractive PEBV. Nonetheless, a scenario analysis shows that if there are no benefits accrued to the firm in terms of greater growth and higher NOPAT margins, the share price, as it is, may prove too high. The conclusion drawn is that Titan America deserves a Hold recommendation.

data.title: Conagra Brands: Don't Rely On This Consumer Defensive For Market Protection
data.description:
Investment Thesis

To say the last few months have been volatile for the stock market would be an understatement. Investors are grappling with the market volatility from major events like “Liberation Day” and increasing geopolitical conflicts.

Investors might assume that Conagra Brands, Inc. (NYSE:CAG), a consumer durable business with a 6.5% dividend yield and recent stock price drop of 28%, now makes it a perfect investment for market protection – but investors would be mistaken.

Conagra Brands has an increasingly weak financial position – a low cash balance with a large debt burden and growing interest expenses. Its operations have similarly deteriorated like its financial position. The company has experienced almost no revenue growth in the two years following its 2018 acquisition of Pinnacle Foods and is still suffering from the supply chain and restructuring costs of the large integration.

I am initiating a Sell rating with an estimated share price of $19.90 and recommend diligent investors avoid the lure of a high dividend yield and the perceived safety attached to a company operating in the consumer defensive sector.

Company Overview

Market Cap

$9.76B

5yr Revenue Growth

2.5%

Enterprise Value

$17.86B

Operating Margin

14.9%

12-month Return (%)

-28.0%

Net Margin

2.8%

Inside Ownership

0.28%

5yr. Dividend CAGR

11.4%

Conagra Brands operates on a fiscal year that ends in May – it will report on its year-end results for 2025 on July 10th, two weeks from now. The company operates an assortment of well-known consumer durable brands with a large shelf presence at most grocery stores across the United States.

Conagra Brands leverages its strong brand presence and shelf space to generate substantial free cash flow – the 2025 TTM free cash flow yield is 13.5% as of the most recent quarter’s results.

A consumer durable business primarily uses its free cash flow to acquire new businesses (inorganic growth through acquisitions), repurchase shares, pay investors a dividend, or pay down debt.

Conagra Brands used its free cash flow to make its most important acquisition in the last decade - the 2018 purchase of Pinnacle Foods. The acquisition was financed through debt and new shares of common stock. The company issued approximately 24% more outstanding shares to finance the acquisition.

Conagra has continued to use its free cash flow to make new acquisitions since its purchase of Pinnacle, but the company has not pursued a strategic share repurchase program. I believe a lack of share repurchases and the issuance of company stock to finance acquisitions implies that Conagra’s management does not believe the company’s share price is undervalued enough to repurchase its common stock.

The company has deployed most of its free cash flow in dividends – the dividend was cut in 2006 but has since grown at a compound annual growth rate of 5.2% through 2024. However, most of the dividend growth came immediately after the Pinnacle acquisition.

Given the five-year stock price decline of 37% and the increase in dividends, investors now have the opportunity to purchase Conagra with a dividend yield of 6.5%, the highest yield in company history. But sustainability and growth of the dividend are far more important than the current yield for diligent investors with a medium to long-term investment horizon, and the balance sheet as of the end of the company’s third quarter 2025 should give investors concern.

At the time of the third quarter earnings call, Conagra had $49.4M of cash on hand, down from $553.3M during mid-2020. The decrease in cash is primarily driven by the large debt servicing of the Pinnacle Foods acquisition and the spike in dividend payments immediately following the acquisition.

Given the company's low liquidity, its large debt balance creates a concern – with over $7.2B in long-term debt as of Q3 ’25, the yearly interest expense is $430M and growing.

Additionally, some long-term notes, held at very advantageous rates, are set to expire soon. Take, for example, the $1B 2027 note at 1.375% - refinancing this note at current rates for a BBB- company would increase Conagra’s yearly interest expense by over $50M.

As more long-term debt at lower weighted average costs of capital comes due, Conagra will be forced to refinance at higher rates. Instead of advantageously using its free cash flow to pay down debt, it continues to issue a growing dividend for its shareholders.

Although I like growing dividends, I like safe and reliable dividends even more – but with the interest expense set to increase from upcoming refinancing needs, and the company’s cash balance near an all-time low, it will become more difficult for management to sustain or increase its existing dividend in my opinion.

The interest on long-term debt is not the only expense I’m worried about – Conagra’s balance sheet shows accumulated depreciation on property, plant & equipment of over 57%. Accumulated depreciation on equipment in the range of 50% to 70% is a good indicator that expenses will increase in the foreseeable future to account for the aging of equipment.

Just like the interest expense, future repair and replacement of equipment, or ‘capital expenditures’, will be higher under an inflationary environment compared to the near-zero rate world prior to 2022. The increase in forecasted expenses over the next several years will likely squeeze Conagra’s free cash flow, making it more difficult for the company to sustain its dividend and pay down debt.

Operations

As a branded consumer packaged goods company, Conagra’s best operational opportunities are inorganic growth (through acquisitions that complement its core offerings) and cost control.

Conagra’s purchase of Pinnacle, mentioned previously, provided strong top-line inorganic growth to the Grocery & Snacks and Refrigerated & Frozen segments of the business in 2019 and 2020. That growth was not sustained – from 2021 through the third quarter of 2025, revenue has only grown at a compound annual growth rate of ~1.2%.

Previous earnings calls have made clear that revenue growth in the last few years has mostly been driven by the macroeconomic headwind in the form of inflationary price raises – not through increased sales volume.

Despite the top-line performance difficulties, management embarked on a multi-year cost efficiency program following the Pinnacle acquisition in an attempt to decrease sales, general, and administrative expenses at the corporate level. Without strong cost control, margins can erode over time, especially following large acquisitions.

Since the announcement of the program in 2019, Conagra has spent over $286M in restructuring costs, supply chain upgrades, etc. to address cost inefficiencies. I am not confident in the results. From 2022 to 2024, sales, general, and administrative expenses increased by 66%, per the SEC annual report filings.

Conagra’s operational performance over the last several years draws concern. The weak top-line growth and inability to reign in core expenses have led to unsatisfactory results.

Valuation

I have implemented a discounted cash flow model to value Conagra and have included assumptions on the projected income statement taken from management expectations, historical performance, and macroeconomic considerations.

Although I have spoken fairly unfavorably of the company’s operational and financial performance, I expect a modest decrease in expenses driven by supply chain normalization and declining inflation. However, I expect most of the cost gains to be offset by declining revenue – I have projected a 2% sequential revenue decline until 2027, the terminal year used in my model.

I have incorporated a weighted average cost of capital using Bruce Greenwald's discount value approach (Greenwald, B. C., Kahn, J. (2011). Value Investing: From Graham to Buffett and Beyond), which focuses on volatility-agnostic underlying business risk. In this scenario, I have used a fixed 8% cost of capital based on Conagra's debt-to-equity structure and terminal growth rate of 1%.

Forecasting slight decreases in the cost of goods sold, and interest expense benefits the net margin substantially. After finishing 2024 with a 2.9% net margin, projecting a 550bps increase is very generous but is more in line with the company’s historical operating performance.

Ultimately, I have arrived at an implied share price of $19.90 and reiterate my Sell rating on Conagra.

There is currently no price scenario that I would find reasonable to make an investment in the company. However, for investors interested in Conagra, I recommend monitoring the company for key changes in operational and financial improvements.

Conagra must find accretive acquisitions that support long-term revenue growth, unlike the recent results from the Pinnacle acquisition. Additionally, the company must obtain better control over its restructuring and sales, general, and administrative costs.

More efforts should be taken to reduce the significant debt burden, and subsequent yearly interest expense, to de-leverage the balance sheet. As mentioned previously, the long-term debt maturity expirations that come due in the next few years will add substantial interest expense, further reducing margins and available free cash flow.

Risks

I have identified two risks to my thesis that could lead to a rating upgrade - one is company-specific while the other relies on positive macroeconomic changes.

First, I have assumed that management will continue to allocate capital in the same manner as in the last few years. If management were to use a larger portion of its extensive free cash flow to pay down higher-yield debt, the company's deleveraged balance sheet would look much stronger.

Financial deleveraging would also greatly benefit operating and net margins from interest expense reduction. For example, if the 2025 forecasted interest expense were to halve, the net margin would increase by 120bps based on my projected income statement shown previously.

The second risk to my bearish thesis is the macroeconomic picture - if household spending were to increase from a reduction in interest rates, Conagra's sales volume would likely increase as robust consumer spending behavior returns. Conagra's valuation would look substantially different if sales volume drove overall revenue growth by, for example, a 3% compound annual growth rate compared with my -2% growth rate.

Conclusion

Consumer durable businesses with high free cash flow generation that reward investors with substantial dividends are great investments during periods of market volatility. Although Conagra currently boasts a 6.5% dividend yield and contains a portfolio of consumer durable businesses, the company carries significant risk.

Conagra’s weak balance sheet – high levels of debt with very little cash on hand – should leave disciplined investors concerned over the safety of future dividend sustainability and growth. Operationally, revenue growth has been outpaced by inflation while the company’s cost controls have not led to a decrease in operating expenses.

I have initiated a Sell rating on Conagra with an implied share price of $19.90 and recommend investors looking for high dividends and business durability consider alternate opportunities.

data.title: Ally Financial: Shares Look Cheap On Easing Cyclical Headwinds
data.description:
Ally Financial (NYSE:ALLY) hasn't enjoyed the best of times of late. On the business front, Detroit-based Ally's recent results are something of a mess, with the firm booking a one-off charge that plunged it into the red last quarter. Its underlying numbers aren't so great either, with the business struggling to earn its cost of equity on the back of some usual cycle-related headwinds. As a result, shares of the car finance giant have lagged the wider U.S. financials space by nearly 30 points over the past 12 months.

I am inclined to think that represents an opportunity for today's prospective buyer. Ally's core results aren't great, but that is par for the course given the cyclical nature of its business. In exchange, shares only trade for 7.2x 2026 consensus EPS – a multiple that I expect will lead to a solid return over the next couple of years.

Earnings Subdued By Typical Cyclical Issues

Ally's asset base skews heavily to retail auto loans. There's over $80 billion worth of them on its balance sheet, mapping to a circa 45% share of its total interest-earning assets. Commercial auto is another $22 billion, or 12%. Even in the context of the wider banking industry, I would say that makes Ally particularly sensitive to the ups and downs of the economic cycle.

Ally's recent financials are admittedly quite messy. Technically, it made a loss of $253 million for its common stockholders last quarter, or $0.82 per share. However, this was distorted by a major one-off, with the bank booking a $495 million pretax loss due to a repositioning of its securities portfolio. Essentially, Ally sold a chunk of low-yielding bonds and repurchased them at prevailing rates, increasing its forward earnings power.

Still, core results are also quite weak. Excluding the above, Ally made around $180 million in net profit for the first quarter. This mapped to a core return on tangible common equity (ROTCE) of just 8.3% using management's definition. Looking back, Ally has averaged a circa 13%-14% core ROTCE in recent times, and it has cleared a double-digit ROTCE in 24 out of the last 30 quarters.

Clearly, the firm is under-earning right now, and this has been caused by typical cycle-related issues. On the revenue side, Ally's lending margin has been squeezed by the industry-wide rise in funding costs, with the bank's reported net interest margin (NIM) clocking in at 3.3% last quarter. This figure is around 20bps below its 5-year average.

The situation is slowly improving. Short-end interest rates have fallen from their peak, and the associated decline in Ally's cost of funds has outpaced the decline in its earning-asset yield. Its NIM was up around 15bps year-on-year last quarter and should continue to improve throughout 2025 and 2026.

Looking at some drivers of that, Ally has a further $26 billion in term deposits maturating over the rest of the year, including around $11 billion this quarter. Per below, the blended yield on its maturing CDs is comfortably over 4%, which is well above Ally's prevailing offering to customers. Management expects full-year NIM of 3.40%-3.50% this year, which also implies a strong comp in 2026.

Credit quality has been another pressing issue for Ally. Delinquencies and net charge-offs are up from their COVID lows, influenced by normalizing used-car prices, which forms the collateral that backs much of its loan book.

There are encouraging signs on this front, too. Net charge-offs landed at 2.12% in the Retail auto-book last quarter, an improvement of 15bps year-on-year. While losses remain elevated compared to their historical average, there is tangible evidence of tighter underwriting standards, with more recent vintages displaying better credit metrics. This should power ongoing improvement in Ally's asset quality in the coming quarters.

As a result of these twin issues, Ally's risk-adjusted interest revenue has been squeezed. Specifically, loan loss provisioning consumed nearly 40% of its net interest revenue last quarter, which is around 15ppt above its five-year average.

Shares Look Cheap

Like all cyclical stocks, timing the rebound is obviously a key consideration, as these issues have plagued Ally for a number of quarters now. However, with NIM expanding and asset quality improving earnings could be about to turn for the firm – a sentiment that is seemingly shared by the sell-side analysts covering the stock. Per Seeking Alpha, they see Ally's quarterly EPS exceeding the $1 mark in the near term, leading to around $5.40 per share in annual EPS by 2026.

Ally trades for $38.78 as I type. The stock does not require an aggressive multiple of these earnings to drive attractive returns. Indeed, a trailing price/earnings ratio of less than 9x would support double-digit annualized share price appreciation over the next couple of years. Including its $0.30 per share quarterly dividend, I expect Ally's total return to comfortably clear its cost of equity.

As an aside, this should also provide a nice tailwind to Ally's capital levels. Currently, Ally's pro forma Common Equity Tier 1 ratio is 9.7%, but this falls to around 7.5% including AOCI losses. That is a little thin for my liking, with the latter only being around 40bps above its required level. As Ally's earnings normalize, its ratio of net income over risk-weighted assets will also improve. Put simply, that will give the firm more scope to build capital, and this will be boosted by the ongoing improvement in its AOCI losses.

Conclusion

Ally Financial has been a relatively weak performer in recent times, as the firm continues to grapple with cyclical headwinds that have squeezed its earnings. Positively, there is tangible evidence that relief is on the horizon, with its NIM expanding and credit quality showing tentative signs of improvement. In turn, this should drive meaningful growth in Ally's earnings in the coming quarters. On a relatively modest multiple, this can power an attractive total return for today's investors. Accordingly, I rate the shares a Buy.

data.title: Compass Diversified: Preferred Stock Is No Less Risky
data.description:
It's not often that I look at a preferred stock Compass Diversified 7.875 CUM PFD C (NYSE:CODI.PR.C), but when it gives a good yield like 10%, it's hard not to be interested, at least initially. Still, Compass Diversified (CODI) has had problems.

The stock is still down somewhat, after taking a plunge in May when the Board of Directors announced an internal investigation of the company's finances. While I think it's more likely the dividends continue to get paid, the not-so-small possibility of bankruptcy deters me from rating it a Buy.

The Disputed Financials

Compass Diversified is a holding company that invests its capital by acquiring small and middle-market businesses. The current holdings (and their business) are:

  • BOA: Footwear
  • Lugano: Jewelry
  • PrimaLoft: Insulation in outerwear
  • The Honey Pot Co.: Feminine hygiene products
  • Velocity Outdoor: Archery and hunting
  • Altor Solutions: Protective foam products
  • Arnold: Magnets and motors
  • Sterno: Food warming, lighting, home fragrance

This is a lot of positions, implying the benefits of diversification. Of course, it's not an even spread.

The table above shows us that, far and away, Lugano is reported to be the biggest contributor to the consolidated financial results. Yet, as the Form 8K that I cited earlier indicated, Lugano was the company being investigated. Preliminary findings concluded that the 2024 financial reports were no longer reliable. It was later reported that the same conclusions were reached for its 2022 and 2023 reports.

As the other holdings operate independently, however, the investigation does not concern them. It's only the financial impact of Lugano that's been under dispute, pertaining to its own accounting practices, namely "sales, cost of sales, inventory, and accounts receivable."

How the Preferred Dividend Fits

As the main concern is with the dividend, let's consider the company's ability to keep paying it.

So far, it hasn't missed, paying its latest distribution in April.

While the reports were said not to be reliable, it's also true that the problems are specific to Lugano, and we can still glean some things from the rest of the consolidated results.

As it currently stands, the entire preferred capitalization (of which the Series C is the most junior) only needs about $25.4M in earnings to meet its preferred dividend obligations.

Recalling that Lugano contributed 21.4% of revenue in 2024 and 57.6% of operating income, that suggests $1,727M in revenue that isn't compromised by the Lugano probe, as well as $97.5M in operating income.

The company only barely has a controlling interest in Lugano, so much more of the remaining operating income is still attributable to the holding company, as opposed to noncontrolling interests.

Where it gets tricky is farther down the income statement, with interest expense exceeding $100M. This narrowly eclipses the operating income that isn't attributable to Lugano. This led the company to adopt measures to improve liquidity, such as suspending the common dividend and entering forbearance with one of its lenders.

Based on that, it seems like the earnings are just on the threshold of either being able to pay the preferred dividends or not. What do we make of that, going forward?

Future Outlook

As the debt issue goes, however, I am mostly not concerned about that. Consider the maturity schedule, for one.

Relatively little of the long-term debt is due in the next couple of years, which I figure is sufficient for the company to sort out the reporting irregularities and figure out what to do with Lugano.

Even if there is a blip in some dividends, the Series C is cumulative, and so these dividends can be recovered in time.

Where I do get concerned is the covenants of the 2022 term loan. The 2024 Form 10-K elaborates:

A breach of any of these covenants will be an event of default under the 2022 Credit Facility. Upon the occurrence of an event of default under the 2022 Credit Facility, the 2022 Revolving Credit Facility may be terminated, and all outstanding loans and other obligations under the 2022 Credit Facility may become immediately due and payable and any letters of credit then outstanding may be required to be cash collateralized, and the Agent and the Lenders may exercise any rights or remedies available to them under the 2022 Credit Facility. Any such event would materially impair the Company’s ability to conduct its business.

This is why there is a forbearance agreement. It's to avoid an immediate default and bankruptcy. If resolving the issues with Lugano results in financial data that violates the covenants, bankruptcy is an imminent threat.

While it appears that the company could liquidate positively, about half the reported assets are goodwill or intangibles. This leaves only about $2B to cover the liabilities, and we know that some tangible assets may have been overstated by Lugano. While the cumulative feature protects against a missed dividend, there's nothing to protect against bankruptcy.

Conclusion

Do I think bankruptcy is particularly likely in this case? No. The company could sell some assets, and maybe even issue more common shares, all while renegotiating the loan at higher interest. It's the fact that bankruptcy is possible that I'm worried about. While I don't write short theses, there's not an appealing buy case here either. For that reason, I give the Series C a Hold rating.

data.title: Enphase: After Rain Comes Sunshine
data.description:
Investor sentiment towards Enphase Energy, Inc. (NASDAQ:ENPH) remains muted in a difficult market environment. Since my previous article, the stock has seen another steep drop. However, Enphase is not the only solar stock that has struggled in 2025. The new proposed US budget bill shows why it is not easy to love solar stocks. If high interest rates and solar panel tariffs were not already enough to consumers, the removal of residential tax credits starting at the end of 2025 could be another hard pill to swallow. Nonetheless, Enphase remains the king of profitability in the stumbling solar sector.

Enphase's operational performance under a magnifying glass

In the graph below, it is visible that Enphase's stock is to some extent fairly punished because of its declining microinverter shipments, which account for the largest share of revenue. In the last two quarters, year-over-year growth returned to microinverter shipments. The following quarters will be important to get full visibility of possible sustainable growth. In the battery segment, the company is performing at record high levels driven by strong demand.

Over the years, there have been some shifts in Enphase's geographical revenue. The main growth stopper for Enphase was interest rates. International revenue is largely impacted by Europe. The United States increased interest rates a lot quicker than Europe, therefore Europe's growth ended two quarters later. Despite the fact that Europe has been cutting rates more aggressively, the solar market has not seen a recovery yet. On the other hand, the US market showed some progress.

Surprisingly, Enphase was able to maintain their strong gross profit and net profit margin, while others in the industry like SolarEdge Technologies, Inc. (SEDG) and SMA Solar Technology AG (OTCPK:SMTGF) saw their margins evaporate. This showcases the strong management team and their focus on efficiencies.

It is good to know that Enphase did receive a US-based advantage which supported their profit margin. The company is eligible for the advanced manufacturing tax credits (45X). This benefit will be phased out over 2030-32, but is still a solid income for the company at the moment.

Valuation and peer performance

Net income and free cash flow were more or less a leading indicator for the stock price. However, this has not been the case in the last 6 months. A gap has arisen between the stock price and free cash flow. In my previous case, I deemed that Enphase was undervalued as the free cash flow would likely recover due to the solar multi-year tailwind. Now the valuation has increasingly become attractive since the free cash flow rebounded and the stock price fell further.

As a result, the stock is currently trading at a very low price to a free cash flow multiple of 10.7x. Net income is still recovering after a negative quarter in 2024 and that's why the P/E ratio is trading at a higher multiple of 33x. Additionally, the company pays out stock-based compensation (a non-cash cost), that influences the gap between net income and free cash flow. To avoid dilution of existing shareholders, the company repurchases its own shares in the open market at favorable prices. The balance sheet can support more share buybacks in the coming quarters with a current ratio of 1.9x and a debt-to-equity ratio of 1.4x.

Furthermore, Enphase is the only cash-generating company from this list of 8 large solar companies. The EV to free cash flow ratio is a valuation metric that measures a company's total value against the cash it generates that is free to be used for dividends, stock buybacks, or debt repayment. This gives Enphase extra flexibility for strategic decisions and survivability until the market environment fully recovers.

Risk

The "one big beautiful bill" act will send shockwaves through the residential solar market if the bill goes live in its current form. The bill is expected to cut the 30% investment tax credit for residential solar projects by the end of 2025. The scope of its impact should be visible rather quickly. However, the Senate might ease these measures as Sen. Kevin Cramer said: "The Senate's cuts to the IRA may ultimately be a little more generous than the House's cuts".

Again in the long run I am not convinced this will stop the solar adoption. Globally, residential solar will continue to grow and renewed demand for high-quality products will return. In addition, a rough market environment could open up new acquisition opportunities for Enphase.

Takeaway

Enphase's valuation is nearing multi-year lows as strong profitability remains despite the decline in sales. The company increased efforts at reducing operational expenditures in the last two years. Those measures include substantial workforce reductions, limited discretionary spending, and the streamlining of its manufacturing footprint.

The demand for residential solar products is likely to rise sooner or later due to the cheap cost and the independency of energy. It is still a large initial investment but a cost-friendly source in the long term.

The disconnect between a cash-generating business and a depressed stock price has favorably increased the risk-reward ratio for either new investors or investors that are averaging down on their position. The upside potential is significant, as I expect the valuation gap to close when the market re-prices the stock based on its resilient free cash flow and market-leading margins. I keep my "Strong Buy" rating on Enphase at $43 a share.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Vinci: A Classic Value Play That's No Longer Undervalued
data.description:
Half a year ago, I wrote my initial analysis on Vinci SA (OTCPK:VCISF) (OTCPK:VCISY), highlighting that this underfollowed company was a bargain trading at just 8.86x P/FCF.

Since then, shares have appreciated by 47.63% and the total return has been 52.46%. Needless to say, that's a great return for just 6 months.

If I were to follow in the footsteps of other analysts, at this point, I'd give myself a pat on the back, highlighting what a genius I am. But that would be false praise.

At the time of writing the article, I expected Vinci to generate low-double digit returns, given the undervaluation and stable business model.

I didn't anticipate that Vinci would start skyrocketing right after I clicked "publish". I also didn't expect the USD to decline rapidly against the EUR, adding to the returns.

While a quick re-rating sometimes happens, that's the exception, not the norm when it comes to value investing. And that's one reason why many investors struggle to generate market-beating returns over the long term: they're not willing to wait, even though time will pass anyway.

Value investing, done right, has been proven to outperform other investing strategies over the long term, as I've experienced myself over the decades. But in order to benefit, we have to be greedy when others are fearful, and fearful when others are greedy.

Vinci's P/E ratio of 14.83 still looks relatively attractive compared to many other choices the markets offer us these days. But the risks I've described in my initial analysis are still there. Essentially, the outlook hasn't changed dramatically since I first covered the company; all that has changed is the price we're paying for the shares.

Momentum investors may scratch their heads, wondering why I'm downgrading Vinci to a "hold", at a time when the shares finally started moving, but if you're a rational investor, it makes sense.

The higher the valuation you pay for Vinci, the more risks you face and the lower your expected returns will be. It's not rocket science, yet many investors seem to struggle with this simple concept, especially in the current exuberant market environment.

Vinci has reached its fair value and as a value investor, who demands a margin of safety, I won't add to my position. Neither am I selling my shares, as I expect the company to keep generating significant free cash flows for the foreseeable future.

Vinci is a Consistent Free Cash Flow Generator

As a long-term investor, I tend to focus on the bigger picture, instead of sweating over quarterly earnings misses. To find great long-term compounders at attractive valuations, I run stock screeners all the time.

My focus is on consistent profitability and low P/FCF ratios. The screener usually shows thousands of stocks, and after careful analysis, less than 1% make it into my personal portfolio. Vinci is such a candidate.

With its "boring" business model, Vinci doesn't make headlines. You won't find any TikTokers shouting the stock is going to the moon. Yet, over the decades, the company has generated consistent returns.

The all-important FCF/Share has been rising quite nicely over the last 10 years, growing by 8.52% a year. As a long-term investor, the only question we need to ask is: will it continue?

As a shareholder, I'd like to see Vinci continue growing at this pace, but it's unlikely.

The first risk is French politicians, who are notorious for putting additional taxes on large corporations like Vinci. But the greater threat is the expiring toll road concessions, as the end of their life is coming closer now.

Vinci's Expiring Concessions Are A Concern

Twenty years ago, the company acquired toll road concessions in France at very attractive valuations. They've got built-in annual toll increases that are linked to the Consumer Price Index (CPI), and offer relatively stable cash flows for us investors. The French toll roads account for roughly 40% of Vinci's annual free cash flow, and they'll mostly expire during 2032-2036. That's an issue.

If Vinci will be able to extend the concessions is far from certain. But it's certain that a potential extension would be costlier than it was twenty years ago. Assets — especially reliable cash flow generators like infrastructure — are in high demand, and it shows in elevated valuations.

Vinci is trying its best to replace the expiring concessions, but the valuations paid, so far, leave me skeptical that the company can achieve its goal.

For a 55-year-long concession of the Budapest airport, the company paid roughly 20x EBITDA. The Edinburgh airport was slightly cheaper at 15x EBITDA. The most expensive acquisition was the Denver Northwest Parkway toll road, a concession that lasts 80 years, and Vinci thought was worth 67x EBITDA. An insane price.

In my previous analysis, I calculated that Vinci could replace roughly 50% to 67% of the expiring concessions with their free cash flow after dividends. This assumes that they keep paying 15 to 20x EBITDA for their acquisitions.

With those numbers in mind, let's do a simplified calculation of how much growth in FCF/share could be achieved until 2037, at the time the largest toll road has expired.

Can Vinci Keep Growing Even With Expiring Concessions?

For this simplified calculation, we're going to use conservative assumptions. We're going to assume 4% growth (roughly in line with inflation) in FCF/share until 2036 and a one-time reduction of 20% in that year.

The 20% reduction comes from the expiring concessions (roughly 40% of FCF) that were partly replaced (50%) by new concessions by then.

Given the higher growth rates achieved during the last decade, a 4% growth rate might seem too conservative, but after a decade of easy money policies, I'd like to factor in a period of lower growth for the coming decade.

I am well aware this calculation isn't as accurate as it could be — since the concessions will expire from 2032 to 2036 and new concessions will be acquired long before that, it also doesn't include potential buybacks, but it's close enough for us to determine the company's potential given the threat of the expiring concessions.

Year: FCF/Share (in Euro):
2024 11.80
2025 12.27
2026 12.76
2027 13.27
2028 13.80
2029 14.36
2030 14.93
2031 15.53
2032 16.15
2033 16.80
2034 17.47
2035 18.17
2036 18.89
2037 15.72

Given those conservative assumptions, FCF/share will rise from €11.80 in 2024 to €15.72 in 2037. That's a CAGR of just 2.23%. After the expiration of the toll roads, I expect Vinci to keep returning to higher growth rates.

Vinci will likely face slowing growth given the challenges, that's why it's important to stay disciplined and only buy into it whenever valuations are low. Right now, at 14.83x earnings and 10.56x P/FCF, the shares seem fully priced, and I downgrade it to a "hold".

Conclusion

Vinci remains a high-quality company with a proven track record of generating reliable free cash flow, but we're no longer looking for a bargain. The stock is fairly valued, as there are meaningful challenges on the horizon.

The market has quickly caught up to what I saw months ago, and from here, future returns will likely be in line with the markets, no longer offering a margin of safety.

I'll continue to hold my position, collect the dividends, and monitor the company's progress in replacing its expiring concessions. But I won't be adding more at current valuations.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
